coredata.dc:title: Diversity of chemiluminescence from fluoroquinolones in the presence of peroxynitrous acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Lijun
Liu, Jing
Liu, Hongcheng
Zhou, Tong
Cai, Hongping
Cheng, Xianglei
coredata.dc:description: In this article, eight fluoroquinolone analogs showed intriguing chemiluminescence (CL) diversity in the presence of peroxynitrous acid. Compared to current literature, these studied fluoroquinolones exhibit many novel characteristics on CL spectra, CL quantum yield and reaction routes. Furthermore, when the distribution of CL was comparable to its fluorescence, a diversity of differences between the analogs were observed. These results indicate that the hydroxyl radical OH and excited state peroxynitrous acid may account for these observations. This discovery could deepen the understanding of mechanisms in CL systems and change the traditional theories about CL phenomena.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemiluminescence
Diversity
Fluoroquinolones
Peroxynitrous acid
Defluorination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the continuous system for CL spectra of FQs. H2O2: 0.80M; perchloric acid: 0.15M; nitrite: 0.20M; FQs: 1.0mM. Flow rate: 1.5mL/min; D: fluorospectrophotometer; F: flow cell; P: peristaltic pump; PC: personal computer; W: waste.
Chemical structure of eight studied FQs.
Spectra of FL and CL of FQs in the present research. (a): FL spectra (left) and CL spectra (right). (b): OFL and SPF. (c): ENX.
Effect of hydroxyl radical ·OH scavengers, tert-butanol, on the ONOOH-FQs system. Concentration of tert-butanol: 0.01M.
Spectra of FL of FQs products in the present research.
The possible mechanisms of ONOOH-FQs CL system.

head.ce:title: Diversity of chemiluminescence from fluoroquinolones in the presence of peroxynitrous acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Quality Standard and Testing Technology, Yunnan Academy of Agriculture Science, 650223 Kunming, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Quality Standard and Testing Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Public Health, Nanchang University, 461 Bayi Road, Nanchang 330006, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Lijun Wei and Jing Liu are co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, eight fluoroquinolone analogs showed intriguing chemiluminescence (CL) diversity in the presence of peroxynitrous acid. Compared to current literature, these studied fluoroquinolones exhibit many novel characteristics on CL spectra, CL quantum yield and reaction routes. Furthermore, when the distribution of CL was comparable to its fluorescence, a diversity of differences between the analogs were observed. These results indicate that the hydroxyl radical OH and excited state peroxynitrous acid may account for these observations. This discovery could deepen the understanding of mechanisms in CL systems and change the traditional theories about CL phenomena.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemiluminescence (CL) is of great importance in many fields [1–6], because of its high sensitivity, low background interference and simple instrumentation. Its application is limited, however, by the slow development of elucidation on the mechanisms of CL reaction. The results of recent research [7,8] on CL reaction indicate that the limit of detection could be improved more than one hundred times when the mechanism of the CL system is successfully discovered.

Fluoroquinolones (FQs), as one of the world’s widely used antibacterial agents in human and veterinary medicine, have been frequently detected in environmental [9,10] and food [11] samples. They lead to a significant increase in drug resistance and can induce a phototoxic and photoallergic reaction, and in some cases even a photomutagenic or phototumorigenic effect, thus posing significant risks on public health.

Methodology for the determination of FQs based on CL detection can be divided into three categories: (1) catalyzing the enhancement of the emission from either the acidic tris(2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium(III) [12] or luminol [13,14] CL system by losing its own electron in the presence of existing substance like oxidants cerium (IV), hydroxyl radical OH and so on; (2) direct oxidation with Ag(III) [15], Mn(IV)-formaldehyde [16] or cerium(IV) [17] and CL emission partly or totally attributed to singlet oxygen 1O2 produced by reaction or the quenching of oxygen; (3) energy transfer from an excited intermediate of the reaction. About half of the CL system on the detection of FQs is explained by energy transfer, such as Ag(III) [15], sulfite [18,19], thiosulfite [20] or hyposulfite [21], lanthanide ion [22,23], peroxyoxalate [24] and peroxynitrous acid (ONOOH) [25], etc.

All the above referenced research present new CL systems and build upon the development of the CL mechanism. Most of these publications, however, focused on single FQs, which may lead to the debate on the CL mechanism. Sun et al. [21] reported the CL systems for five FQs and proposed that the emission at 353 and 366nm resulted from excited sulfur dioxide SO2 * and excited state norfloxacin (NOR) oxide based on the distribution of photoluminescence and CL spectra. But there is a substantial divergence of CL emission in the work proposed by Xie et al. [20] using similar CL systems and experimental methods, who regarded Ce(III)* as the luminophore at 360nm.

ONOOH is a strong oxidant and its synthesis could be readily performed by mixing acidic hydrogen peroxide and nitrite in a simple flow device. Song and coworkers [25] report the determination of three FQs based on the CL reaction of ONOOH with FQs and collected the CL spectra by using interference filters. A mechanism involving energy transfer from ONOOH* leading to emission of isomeration of ciprofloxacin (CPX) is proposed. However, the results from Francis et al. [26] indicate that the CL results based on interference filter would distort the real spectral distribution. Moreover, Song and coworkers only show the CL spectra of CPX in their research work.

Unlike other CL approaches for determination of FQs, ONOOH-FQs is superior in terms of its simplicity, environmentally friendly products and the absence of a significant background. Therefore, the ONOOH-FQs CL system was selected for identifying the CL spectra of FQs. In this article, eight FQ analogs were investigated based on the ONOOH-CL system. Under the same condition, CL emissions were observed for all eight tested FQs. Interestingly, CL diversity of the reactions between ONOOH and different FQs was found in the present study.

A flow-injection analysis manifold consisting of an IFIS-A CL detector biased at −800V and a RFL-I data analyzer was used for the recording of CL intensity. A schematic diagram of the continuous CL system used in this work is shown in Fig. 1 . Regent solutions were delivered by a peristaltic pump (Baoding Longer Precision Pump Co. Ltd., China). For the collection of CL spectra, the excitation source of the fluorospectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent, USA) was turned off (Bio/Chemiluminescence mode). PTFE tubing (0.8mm inner diameter) was used to connect all components in the flow system. First, the nitrite solution (0.20M) was mixed with acidic hydrogen peroxide solution (0.8M) through a PEFE Y-piece. Then a second Y-piece was used to merge the solution with one of tested FQs prior to the final confluence point. A quartz spiral flow cell (0.5mm i. d.) was mounted in front of the emission window of the fluorospectrophotometer. Solutions were pumped at flow rates of 1.5ml/min (per line). Spectra were an average of ten scan (1s gate time, 1nm data interval).

Acidic hydrogen peroxide solution was prepared by dissolving H2O2 (30%, AR, Shanghai Jinlu Chemical Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) in a perchloric acid (GR, Shanghai Taopu Chemical Factory, Shanghai, China) solution daily. Working sodium nitrite solutions were prepared in ultrapure water from NaNO2 (AR, Shantou Xinglong Chemical Factory, Guangdong, China) on a daily basis. NOR, CPX, ofloxacin (OFL), pefloxacin (PEF), fleroxacin (FLX), lomefloxacin (LOM), sparfloxacin (SPF), and enoxacin (ENX) were purchased from the National Institute for the Control of Pharmaceutical and Biological Products (Beijing, China). The chemical structures of the eight studied FQs are shown in Fig. 2 . The solution of 1.0mM of eight studied FQs were prepared by dissolved appropriate amount of these studied FQs in 0.5ml ice acetic acid and diluted to 50ml with ultrapure water separately.

To investigate the nature of emitting species in the present system, CL spectra using the continuous flow manifold (Fig. 1) was collected. It was very interesting that spectral distribution of each FQ contained a single maximum locat approximately around 500nm (Fig. 3 a), which indicated that the light emitterspossessed similar structure. It is noteworthy that the distribution of CL emission did not match the respective fluorescence (FL). When comparing with the maximum wavelength of fluorescent emission, the rule of differences (c.a. 50nm) between CL and FL wavelength was obvious. The observed differences in the CL spectra of ONOOH-FQs and the spectra of FL further proved that emitters in the present system were the excited products of FQs, but not the excited FQs* claimed by Song and coworkers [25]. The wavelength shift of 50nm represented could be a result of improved conjugation effect of quinolone ring. Some research results regarding the degradation of FQs [27] and sulfa [28] implicate that the fluorine atom in position 6 was substituted by a hydroxyl group, which would result in a remarkable shift to a longer wavelength of approximately 50–100nm.

Furthermore, experimental results illustrate that FLX (Fig. 3a) and ENX (Fig. 3c) had the maximal CL intensity among all the studied FQs. As mentioned above, ONOOH-FQs CL possibly passed through the process of substitution of the fluorine atom by a hydroxyl group to give the corresponding 6-hydroxyquinolone. Hence, the quantum yield of the present CL system was proportional to the electrophilicity of the heterocyclic ring, which is mainly determined by the group in position 8. Consistent with this mechanism, it is parallel to the difference in electrophilicity induced by the substituent group in position 8, such as electron withdrawing aza substitution in ENX and fluorine atom in FLX.

Where did the hydroxyl radical come from? The reaction of nitrite with hydrogen peroxide in acidic medium has been studied in detail [29–32]. As a result, ONOOH was generated and could exist in different forms, such as cis-ONOOH, trans-ONOOH, ONOOH*. Fortunately, a part of ONOOH could undergo hemolytic fission to generate hydroxyl (OH) and NO2 radicals. Previous studies [27] indicated that the addition of OH to the carbon fluorine position lead to a germinal fluorohydrin intermediate that undergoes rapid HF elimination. Likewise, displacement of fluorine also improved the stability of quinolones [33]. In order to further investigate the vital role of hydroxyl radicals, tert-butyl alcohol [34,35] was also used as a scavenger. As shown in Fig. 4 , the results indicated that OH played a key role in the present ONOOH-FQs CL system. Additionally, our preliminary investigations on the mixture of FQs and ONOOH showed that the CL intensity of ONOOH-FQs was linear with the concentration of fluoride. Thehe identification of the intermediate products is in progress.

There are eight FQs in the present literature, among them OFL was the most used in the market. Since there was a six-member ring between position 1 and position 8 of quinolone, OFL was studied for the collection of ONOOH-FQs CL spectra as shown in Fig. 3b. The experimental results indicated a single broad emission with a maximum intensity at 505nm. There was good consistency in maximum wavelength between CL and FL as for OFL. Obviously, these results could not be explained by the substitution of the fluorine atom at position 6 by the hydroxyl group and different reactions would occur. There was much less chance of deflourination taking place in position 6 since OFL bares an additional electron donating alkoxyl group at position 8. Furthermore, the minimal effect of OH scavenger on the CL intensity of OFL (Fig. 4) suggested a different mode for ONOOH-FQs.

Similarly, the same chemistry was found with SPF. As for SPF, the presence of a second fluorine in position 8 made the molecule much more reactive through electrophilicity, taking for instance, FLX and ENX. However, the CL intensity of ONOOH in the presence of SPF was minimal among the eight studied FQs (Fig. 2b). In addition, the CL spectral distribution of SPF was quite in accord with its photoluminescence as illustrated in Fig. 2b. These results indicated that the electron donor amino group of SPF at position 5 eliminated the facilitation of defluorination by an electron-withdrawing effect and 6-hydroxyquinolone was not formed as a result.

To further elucidate the CL mechanisms, FL experiments of products generated from ONOOH-FQs CL reaction were carefully carried out. Fortunately, expected products baring similar wavelength characteristics corresponding to CL distribution were detected, as shown in Fig. 5 . The results indicated that compared to themselves, the products shifted to red band about 50nm for CPX, ENX, FLX, LOM, NOR, and PEF. However, as for OFL and SPF, new emission peaks have not been found in the present work.

On the other hand, it was well known that the greater the degree of overlap of the luminescence spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor, the greater was the probability that energy transfer would take place. Based on the data from references [36,37], the decomposition of ONOOH* to nitrate is exothermic and ΔE should be approximately 77kcalmol−1. Calculations indicated that the emission wavelength of this reaction was about 370nm. Hence, it was deduced that energy transfer took place for OFL and SPF. Why did energy transfer not occur in other FQs? Actual data proved that excited FQ molecules could not be adequately produced yet by this energy because excited wavelength is about 280nm for most of the studied FQs, except OFL (299 and 330nm) and SPF (370nm). ENX with excited wavelength at 344nm did not accept the energy released from ONOOH*, which further manifested that chemical structure of FQs played an important role in the ONOOH-FQs system.
On the basis of the above studies, mechanisms of ONOOH-FQs CL system could be speculated as thefollowing reactions represented graphically in Fig. 6 .

First, HNO2 reacted with H2O2 via nucleophilic substitution to generate cis-ONOOH, which could partly convert into its trans form, trans-ONOOH [37]. Then trans-ONOOH could further rearrange and decompose to produce OH and NO2 radicals (reaction 1 in Fig. 6) or change into its isomer ONOOH* (reaction 2 in Fig. 6) [37,38].

As for most of FQs studied including CPX, ENX, FLX, LOM and NOR, the hydroxyl radical OH as a strong oxidant reacted with FQs to give excited 6-hydroxyquinolones. The CL emission was observed with the decay of excited 6-hydroxyquinolones (reaction 3 in Fig. 6).
On the other hand, ONOOH* transferred its energy to FQ molecules (OFL and SPF) to give FQ* which returned back to the ground state and, as a result, CL took place (reaction 4 in Fig. 6).

In summary, CL diversity of the ONOOH-FQs system with attractive phenomenon was presented here. Different distribution of CL spectra, discrepancies between CL and FL spectra, CL quantum yield as well as a different way of CL emission were found. The possible CL mechanisms were also proposed in this article. Further detailed research with regard to ONOOH-FQs CL is under investigation in our laboratory.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank for the financial supports from the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21265013 and 81260435), Social development project of Yunnan province (2013RA012) and the Innovation Fund Designated for Graduate Students of Nanchang University (cx2015187).
